items.title: Use Vector Dot/Cross to Avoid Angle Misidentification
items.description: Compute \(\tan\) of an angle via cross/dot products instead of relying on which “opposite/adjacent” leg a diagram suggests.
items.content: When the angle’s right-triangle decomposition is ambiguous, represent the angle using two rays as vectors and use \(\tan\theta=\frac{\|\vec u\times \vec v\|}{\vec u\cdot \vec v}\) (with correct absolute/sign handling). This is harder to misapply than splitting angles into smaller right triangles and prevents systematic geometry mistakes.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converge on a robust coordinate/vector method: place the altitude foot and the two base points so the altitude segments fix the x-coordinates, then compute the tangent of the angle at the vertex using a general formula involving dot and cross products. Unsuccessful attempts largely fail by mis-identifying which right triangle pieces correspond to the given angle, then forcing tangent addition or opposite/adjacent ratios with inconsistent geometry; some also get stuck in “AIME must be integer” interpretation without actually re-deriving the correct relation. The key differentiators are (1) using a representation where the given segment lengths directly determine coordinates, (2) computing tan(angle) in a coordinate-invariant way (cross/dot), and (3) validating the algebraic relation carefully (especially signs in the dot product/cross product formula) rather than retrofitting to an expected answer.

items.title: Fix a Coordinate System from Given Lengths
items.description: Place points so the given segment lengths become direct coordinate differences, then compute the unknown using geometry/algebra.
items.content: Choose coordinates where the altitude foot is at the origin and the base segment splits correspond exactly to x-coordinates; this turns length data into immediate coordinate constraints. It reduces the chance of mixing up which segment is adjacent/opposite because everything is derived from the same placement.

items.description: After any transformation (sign flips, tangent addition, slope formulas), re-check against the original definition of the target angle.

items.description: A guessed AIME/AoPS-style integrality requirement should not replace correct derivation of the governing equation.
items.content: When a result is non-integer, treat it as evidence the angle relation was set up incorrectly, not as permission to reinterpret formulas ad hoc.
